verses.commentary: Do not rebuke an older man harshly qualifies correction method. But exhort him as if he were your father provides alternative: appeal, not attack. Treat younger men as brothers extends family metaphor. Ministry relationships mirror family dynamics: respect for age, equality with peers.

Older women as mothers, and younger women as sisters continues family model. With absolute purity adds critical qualifier for cross-gender ministry. Purity guards against sexual sin in pastoral relationships.

Give proper recognition to those widows who are really in need begins extended section on widow care. Proper recognition suggests both honor and material support. Really in need distinguishes genuine from those with family resources.

But if a widow has children or grandchildren identifies those with family. These should learn first of all to put their religion into practice by caring for their own family connects theology to obligation. Repaying their parents and grandparents names the debt. For this is pleasing to God grounds family care in divine approval.

The widow who is really in need and left all alone describes genuine widow. Puts her hope in God and continues night and day to pray characterizes her devotion. Destitution plus devotion qualifies for church support.
But the widow who lives for pleasure is dead even while she lives describes spiritual death in physical life. Self-indulgent living forfeits both support and spiritual vitality.
Give the people these instructions commands Timothy to teach these principles. So that no one may be open to blame states purpose: instruction prevents accusation against church and members.

Anyone who does not provide for their relatives states universal principle. And especially for their own household intensifies obligation to immediate family. Has denied the faith and is worse than an unbeliever makes shocking claim: family neglect contradicts faith more flagrantly than unbelief. Practical love validates professed belief.

No widow may be put on the list of widows establishes official enrollment. Unless she is over sixty sets age requirement. Has been faithful to her husband adds marital fidelity requirement. The list created formal support structure with qualifying criteria.

And is well known for her good deeds adds reputation requirement. Such as bringing up children, showing hospitality, washing the feet of the Lord's people, helping those in trouble and devoting herself to all kinds of good deeds catalogs qualifying works. Past faithfulness predicts future character.

As for younger widows, do not put them on such a list excludes those under sixty. For when their sensual desires overcome their dedication to Christ, they want to marry explains rationale. Young widows face stronger remarriage desires that might compromise commitment to Christ.

Thus they bring judgment on themselves, because they have broken their first pledge suggests enrollment involved vow. Breaking commitment incurs guilt. The passage assumes pledges of devotion that remarriage would violate.

Besides, they get into the habit of being idle and going about from house to house adds practical concern. And not only do they become idlers, but also busybodies who talk nonsense, saying things they ought not to describes three vices: idleness, meddling, gossip. Unstructured time produces destructive behavior.

So I counsel younger widows to marry, to have children, to manage their homes recommends remarriage and domestic focus. And to give the enemy no opportunity for slander states purpose: avoid giving Satan ammunition for accusation against the church.
Some have in fact already turned away to follow Satan reports actual apostasy. The danger was not theoretical but realized. Some young widows had already defected.

If any woman who is a believer has widows in her care shifts responsibility. She should continue to help them and not let the church be burdened with them preserves church resources. So that the church can help those widows who are really in need focuses limited resources on the truly destitute.

The elders who direct the affairs of the church well are worthy of double honor transitions to elder support. Especially those whose work is preaching and teaching singles out teaching elders. Double honor likely means both respect and financial remuneration.

For Scripture says introduces two quotations. Do not muzzle an ox while it is treading out the grain cites Deuteronomy 25:4. The worker deserves his wages quotes Jesus (Luke 10:7). Both support paying ministers. Old Testament law and Jesus' teaching converge.

Do not entertain an accusation against an elder unless it is brought by two or three witnesses protects leaders from slander. Multiple witnesses requirement guards against false charges while allowing legitimate ones.

But those elders who are sinning you are to reprove before everyone requires public rebuke for persistent sin. So that the others may take warning states purpose: deterrence. Public sin demands public correction to maintain holiness and warn others.

I charge you, in the sight of God and Christ Jesus and the elect angels invokes solemn witnesses: divine and angelic. To keep these instructions without partiality commands impartial application. And to do nothing out of favoritism reinforces impartiality. Justice requires treating all equally.

Do not be hasty in the laying on of hands warns against premature ordination. And do not share in the sins of others explains consequence: complicity through bad appointments. Keep yourself pure adds personal holiness requirement. Hasty ordination implicates the ordainer in subsequent sin.

Stop drinking only water, and use a little wine because of your stomach and your frequent illnesses appears to be parenthetical medical advice. Timothy's asceticism was harming his health. Moderate wine use for medicinal purposes is permitted.

The sins of some are obvious, reaching the place of judgment ahead of them describes public sin. The sins of others trail behind them describes hidden sin eventually exposed. Time reveals character; some immediately, others eventually.

In the same way, good deeds are obvious applies principle to virtue. And even those that are not obvious cannot remain hidden forever promises eventual revelation. Time exposes both sin and righteousness. Character eventually surfaces.
